note_data.title: Electrochemistry
keyPoints: **Oxidation** occurs at the anode where electrons are lost and **reduction** occurs at the cathode where electrons are gained
**Standard electrode potential** (E°) is measured under standard conditions: 298K, 100 kPa, 1 mol dm⁻³ concentration
The **standard hydrogen electrode** is assigned a potential of 0.00 V and acts as the reference electrode
**Cell EMF** is calculated using E°(cell) = E°(positive terminal) - E°(negative terminal)
A **positive EMF** indicates the reaction is feasible under standard conditions
During **electrolysis**, cations migrate to the cathode and anions migrate to the anode
The amount of substance produced during electrolysis is proportional to the **quantity of electricity** passed (Faraday's laws)
**Rechargeable cells** have reversible electrode reactions while primary cells have irreversible reactions

definitions.definition: The EMF of a half-cell measured against the standard hydrogen electrode under standard conditions
The decomposition of an ionic compound by the passage of electricity
Electromotive force - the maximum potential difference between two electrodes when no current flows

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Show all working, include units, use appropriate number of significant figures
Use electrode potentials to determine feasibility or products formed

modelAnswer.question: Calculate the standard cell EMF for the reaction: Zn(s) + Cu²⁺(aq) → Zn²⁺(aq) + Cu(s). Use the standard electrode potentials: Zn²⁺/Zn = -0.76 V, Cu²⁺/Cu = +0.34 V. State whether this reaction is feasible.
modelAnswer.answer: E°(cell) = E°(positive terminal) - E°(negative terminal) = (+0.34) - (-0.76) = +1.10 V. The reaction is feasible because the EMF is positive.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Correct formula or identification of positive and negative terminals
Mark 3: States feasible because EMF is positive

commonMistakes: Never say 'electrons flow from cathode to anode' — electrons always flow from anode to cathode in the external circuit
Students often confuse electrode signs in electrolytic vs galvanic cells — anode is always where oxidation occurs
Never subtract electrode potentials the wrong way round — always subtract the more negative from the more positive
Students forget to state standard conditions when defining standard electrode potential
Never say 'current flows' without specifying direction — conventional current flows from positive to negative terminal
examinerTips: Electrode potential calculations appear in almost every Paper 2 exam
Always check the sign of your EMF calculation — negative means not feasible under standard conditions
Learn the standard conditions by heart: 298K, 100 kPa, 1 mol dm⁻³
For electrolysis questions, identify what is being oxidized and reduced at each electrode
Use the electrochemical series data sheet provided in exams
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Electrochemical cell showing two half-cells connected by salt bridge, with electron flow in external circuit from anode to cathode, and ion movement in salt bridge to complete the circuit
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to explain the process of electroplating copper onto a steel spoon, including electrode reactions and setup
N/D 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — multiple choice question on identifying which substance would be produced at the cathode during electrolysis of molten sodium chloride
M/J 2022 P2 Q6 (8 marks) — comprehensive question on electrolysis of copper sulfate solution using different electrodes, requiring electrode equations and observations
N/D 2023 P2 Q3 (5 marks) — asked students to predict products of electrolysis of dilute sulfuric acid and explain why hydrogen is produced instead of sodium at the cathode

workedExample.question: Describe what happens during the electrolysis of molten lead bromide. Include the electrode reactions and explain why the electrolyte must be molten. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: State that lead bromide must be molten to allow ions to move freely and conduct electricity
Step 2: Identify that Pb²⁺ ions move to cathode (negative electrode) and Br⁻ ions move to anode (positive electrode)
Step 3: Write cathode reaction: Pb²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Pb (reduction - gain of electrons)
Step 4: Write anode reaction: 2Br⁻ → Br₂ + 2e⁻ (oxidation - loss of electrons)
Step 5: State observations: grey metallic lead forms at cathode, orange/brown bromine gas at anode
workedExample.answer: Lead bromide must be molten so that the ions can move freely to conduct electricity. During electrolysis, Pb²⁺ ions move to the cathode where they gain electrons: Pb²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Pb. Grey metallic lead is deposited. Br⁻ ions move to the anode where they lose electrons: 2Br⁻ → Br₂ + 2e⁻. Orange/brown bromine gas is produced.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for molten explanation, 2 marks for correct electrode reactions with electrons shown, 1 mark for ion movement, 2 marks for correct observations

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused oxidation and reduction at electrodes' — always remember OIL RIG: Oxidation Is Loss (of electrons, occurs at anode), Reduction Is Gain (of electrons, occurs at cathode)
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently forgot to balance equations and omit electron symbols' — always include electrons (e⁻) in electrode equations and ensure charges balance on both sides

Examiner Report 2023: 'Candidates often gave products for molten compounds when asked about aqueous solutions' — remember that with aqueous solutions, H⁺ and OH⁻ from water may be discharged instead of metal ions
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw an electrolysis cell showing: a d.c. power supply with positive and negative terminals clearly marked, two electrodes (anode connected to positive, cathode to negative), electrolyte solution/molten compound, direction of electron flow in external circuit (from anode to cathode), direction of ion movement (cations to cathode, anions to anode), and labels for all components. Include gas collection tubes if gases are produced.
note_data.quickRecap: Electrolysis uses electricity to decompose ionic compounds - ions move to oppositely charged electrodes where they gain or lose electrons. Cations go to cathode (reduction), anions go to anode (oxidation). Remember OIL RIG for electron transfer. In aqueous solutions, H⁺ and OH⁻ from water may be discharged instead of metal ions depending on reactivity.
